assertions.text: The pay table includes intermediate expected columns (e.g. Regular pay?, Overtime pay?, Premium pay?) so the reader can trace which rule contributes what — not just a single total Weekly pay? column.
assertions.evidence: The table header is: `Scenario | Weekday hours | Sunday hours | Holiday hours | Rate | Weekly pay?` — there is only a single `Weekly pay?` output column; no intermediate columns like `Regular pay?`, `Overtime pay?`, or `Premium pay?` are present.

assertions.text: The error/rejection table has an expected column (e.g. 'Throws?' or 'Exception?') specifying the exception type per row — rather than hardcoding the exception type in @Description or the method name.
assertions.evidence: The error table header is `Scenario | Rate` with no expected exception column. The exception type is hardcoded in the method body: `assertThrows(IllegalArgumentException.class, () -> calculator.calculate(0, 0, 0, rate));`

assertions.text: Overtime threshold boundary is covered: at least a value at the threshold (40) and just above (41 or similar). Not just values well below and well above.
assertions.evidence: The table contains `Standard 40-hour week | 40 | 0 | 0 | 10.00 | 400.00` and `One overtime hour | 41 | 0 | 0 | 10.00 | 415.00`, covering exactly the threshold and one step above.

assertions.text: A combined scenario is present: a row with weekday + Sunday + holiday hours, testing the composition of all hour types.
assertions.evidence: `Overtime with Sunday and holiday | 48 | 4 | 2 | 10.00 | 640.00` — this row has all three hour types: 48 weekday, 4 Sunday, 2 holiday.

assertions.text: Error/edge cases covered: at least negative hours (floored at zero) and negative or missing rate (rejected).
assertions.evidence: Only negative rate is tested (`Negative rate | {-10.00, -1.00, -0.01}`). There is no test for negative hours. The design notes explicitly explain this omission: 'The zero floor is covered by @Description rather than a dedicated row, since valid non-negative inputs can never produce negative pay under these rules — no dedicated test scenario is possible without introducing negative hours, which the spec doesn't define.'

assertions.text: Sunday and Holiday hour columns use empty cells (not 0) when those inputs are not relevant to a scenario — making it easier to spot which rows involve weekend/holiday hours.
assertions.evidence: Rows like `Standard 40-hour week | 40 | 0 | 0 | 10.00 | 400.00` and `One overtime hour | 41 | 0 | 0 | 10.00 | 415.00` use `0` (not blank) in the Sunday and Holiday columns when those hour types are not relevant to the scenario.

assertions.text: Scenario names describe work patterns (e.g. 'Part-time', 'Five hours overtime', 'Full Sunday shift') — not 'Test case 1' or expected outcomes.
assertions.evidence: Scenario names include: 'No hours worked', 'Zero rate', 'Standard 40-hour week', 'One overtime hour', 'Eight overtime hours', 'Sunday hours only', 'Holiday hours only', 'Full week with Sunday', 'Overtime with Sunday and holiday' — all describe work patterns.

assertions.text: Expected values in intermediate and total columns are arithmetically correct for every row, consistent with the formulas in @Description.
assertions.evidence: Verified against formulas: `Standard 40-hour week`: 40×10=400.00 ✓; `One overtime hour`: 40×10+1×1.5×10=415.00 ✓; `Eight overtime hours`: 40×10+8×1.5×10=520.00 ✓; `Sunday hours only`: 8×2×10=160.00 ✓; `Full week with Sunday`: 400+160=560.00 ✓; `Overtime with Sunday and holiday`: 400+120+80+40=640.00 ✓. No intermediate columns exist to verify.

assertions.text: Value sets are only used where all values in the set produce the same expected result (e.g. any rate × 0 = 0). Not used where results would differ.
assertions.evidence: The only value set is `{-10.00, -1.00, -0.01}` used in the rejection table — all three values are negative rates that violate the same rule and all produce the same outcome (IllegalArgumentException). The design notes confirm: 'all three must throw because they all violate the same rule.'

assertions.text: @DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.
assertions.evidence: No @DisplayName on individual test methods, but the method names `calculatesWeeklyPay` and `rejectsNegativeHourlyRate` read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase: 'calculates weekly pay' and 'rejects negative hourly rate'.

assertions.text: @Description is present on at least the payable hours method and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the conversion rules, fixed values, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: @Description on `calculatesWeeklyPay` states: `"Weekly pay = (weekday hours up to 40) × rate + (weekday hours beyond 40) × 1.5 × rate [overtime] + sunday hours × 2 × rate [double time] + holiday hours × 2 × rate [double time]. Total pay is floored at zero."` — this explains the conversion formulas and multipliers, which go beyond restating column names.

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
assertions.evidence: For both test methods the order is `@Description` then `@TableTest` (no `@DisplayName` on methods), which matches the required order when @DisplayName is absent: `@Description("...") @TableTest("""...""") void calculatesWeeklyPay(...)` and `@Description("...") @TableTest("""...""") void rejectsNegativeHourlyRate(...)`.

assertions.text: @Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: The @Description on `calculatesWeeklyPay` uses string concatenation: `"Weekly pay = (weekday hours up to 40) × rate" + " + (weekday hours beyond 40) × 1.5 × rate  [overtime]" + " + sunday hours × 2 × rate                  [double time]" + " + holiday hours × 2 × rate                 [double time]." + " Total pay is floored at zero."` — this is multiple strings joined with `+`, not a triple-quoted text block.
